nativeRange.habitat: Still or slow-moving freshwater bodies including ponds, ditches, paddies/rice fields, and the shallow margins of lakes and slow rivers

decorPreferences: any substrate or no substrate (tolerates floating)
dense planted thickets or surface mats for fry and shrimp refuge

summary: A fast-growing fine-leaved Asian annual stem/floating plant from the Hydrocharitaceae; widely sold as Guppy Grass or Indian Naiad and prized as a fry and shrimp refuge in breeding tanks.
careNotes: Najas indica is a fast-growing annual aquatic plant from still or slow-moving freshwaters of India, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, and New Guinea. In the aquarium trade it is most often sold under the names 'Guppy Grass' (a name shared with the New-World Najas guadalupensis — the two are often used interchangeably by retailers and the species sold as 'Guppy Grass' or 'Najas Roraima' is not always identified to species) and 'Indian Naiad'. It can be grown either as a loose stem plant pushed gently into substrate or simply left floating — both forms generally produce dense thickets within weeks under low-to-moderate light without CO2 supplementation. Generally advised to keep around 22–28 °C; tolerates a wide range of pH (≈ 6.0–7.5) and hardness, and adapts to most aquarium conditions, which is the principal reason it is recommended for beginner and breeding setups. Stems are brittle and snap easily, which is the main propagation route: any broken fragment with a node will continue growing. This same brittleness is what makes the plant excellent fry and shrimp refuge — the dense, tangled, fine-leaved mass provides countless hiding spaces for newborn livebearers, killifish, and shrimplets — but also means it is INVASIVE in some regions outside its native range (notably parts of the Mediterranean, southern Europe, and waterways near aquarium-trade hubs); aquarists should never release trimmings or unwanted material into local waterways and should compost or dispose of cuttings in household waste. No fertilisation, special substrate, or CO2 is required for healthy growth; supplemental ferts will increase growth rate but are rarely necessary. The plant is a column feeder. Regular trimming is generally needed to keep it from completely overtaking a tank.
breedingNotes: Propagated almost trivially by stem fragmentation — any cutting or broken fragment with at least one node will continue growing whether floated or planted. Lateral side-shoots from established stems can also be separated and replanted. The brittle stems mean propagation often happens spontaneously as a side effect of routine maintenance.

primary.name: Wikipedia
primary.notes: Used for taxonomy (Hydrocharitaceae, Alismatales), native range (India, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, New Guinea), habitat (still/slow-moving waters including ponds and rice fields), annual life cycle, and synonym (Caulinia indica). Article is a stub and contains no husbandry-specific data.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry: low-to-moderate light, no CO2 needed, fast growth, can be planted or left floating, height 4–12 in (≈10–30 cm), pH 6.0–7.5, easy care, propagation via stem cuttings, no fertilisation required. Also confirms trade names Guppy Grass and Indian Naiad.
